title: Modal Verbs (Modalverben)
description: Modal verbs modify the meaning of the main verb, expressing ability, permission, obligation, desire, or necessity. German has six modal verbs: können, müssen, dürfen, sollen, wollen, and mögen (with its subjunctive form möchten). They follow special conjugation patterns and require a specific word order.
whenToUse: Expressing ability or possibility: Ich kann schwimmen.
Expressing obligation or necessity: Du musst deine Hausaufgaben machen.
Expressing permission or prohibition: Hier darf man nicht rauchen.
Expressing a recommendation or duty: Du sollst mehr Wasser trinken.
Expressing a wish or desire: Ich will Arzt werden. / Ich möchte einen Kaffee.

rules.title: Word Order: Modal Verb in Position 2, Infinitive at the End
rules.explanation: In a main clause, the modal verb is conjugated and takes position 2. The main verb stays in the infinitive form and goes to the very end of the sentence.

examples.explanation: The infinitive (sprechen) must go to the end of the clause, after all other elements.
The modal (musst) is in position 2, and the infinitive (aufstehen) is at the end.

examples.correct: Wir wollen nächstes Jahr nach Japan fliegen.
examples.explanation: Even with many elements between them, the modal stays in position 2 and the infinitive at the end.

rules.title: Conjugation Pattern: Singular Forms Are Irregular
rules.explanation: Modal verbs have irregular singular forms — the ich and er/sie/es forms are identical and have no ending. The plural forms follow regular patterns. Most modals also change their stem vowel in the singular.

examples.explanation: The ich-form of können is kann (not könne, which is Konjunktiv I). No ending is added.
The er/sie/es form is identical to the ich-form: ich muss, er muss (not musst — that is du).
Plural forms are regular: wir dürfen, ihr dürft, sie dürfen.

rules.title: Negation with nicht
rules.explanation: To negate a sentence with a modal verb, nicht is typically placed before the infinitive at the end. If there is no infinitive, nicht follows the modal verb.

examples.explanation: Nicht goes directly before the infinitive: nicht rauchen.
Nicht is placed before the infinitive kommen.
When the infinitive is sein, nicht comes before it as usual.

rules.title: möchten vs. wollen
rules.explanation: Both express wanting, but möchten is the polite form (would like) and wollen is the direct form (want). Use möchten in polite contexts, restaurants, and with strangers. Wollen can sound demanding if used with people.

examples.explanation: In a restaurant or shop, möchten is polite. Wollen sounds rude or demanding in this context.
For strong personal goals and ambitions, wollen is appropriate: I want to become a doctor.
Use möchten for polite invitations and offers.

rules.title: Meanings of Each Modal Verb
rules.explanation: Each modal verb has a core meaning: können (ability/possibility), müssen (necessity/obligation), dürfen (permission), sollen (recommendation/duty/hearsay), wollen (strong desire/intention), möchten (polite wish).

examples.correct: Du sollst nicht so viel Zucker essen.
examples.explanation: Sollen expresses a recommendation or instruction from someone else: You should not eat so much sugar.
Dürfen expresses permission — or, with nicht, prohibition: One is not allowed to take photos here.
Sollen can express hearsay — something someone heard: It is supposed to rain tomorrow.

tables.title: Modal Verb Conjugation in Present Tense
Modal Verb Meanings at a Glance

commonMistakes.incorrect: Ich will nach Hause zu gehen.
commonMistakes.explanation: Modal verbs take a bare infinitive without 'zu'. Never add zu before the infinitive with a modal verb.
The er/sie/es form is the same as ich: er muss (not musst, which is the du-form).
In polite situations (restaurants, requests), use möchten. Wollen sounds demanding.

commonMistakes.incorrect: Du musst das nicht zu machen.
commonMistakes.explanation: No zu before the infinitive with modal verbs. This is a common transfer error from English.
Nicht goes before the infinitive: nicht schwimmen, not after it.
Sollen (should, someone recommends) is softer than müssen (must, absolute necessity). Choose based on intended strength.

tips: Modal verbs never take 'zu' before the infinitive. This is one of the most reliable rules in German grammar.
The ich-form and er/sie/es-form are always identical for modal verbs: ich kann = er kann, ich muss = er muss.
müssen + nicht means 'don't have to' (no obligation), NOT 'must not'. For prohibition, use dürfen + nicht: Du darfst das nicht tun.
In the Präteritum, modal verbs lose their umlaut: können → konnte, müssen → musste, dürfen → durfte.
möchten is technically the Konjunktiv II of mögen, but it functions as its own modal verb meaning 'would like'.
